Frontend interviews in 2026 have quietly split into two halves.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Top 15 Frontend Interview Questions for 2026
Frontend interviews in 2026 have quietly split into two halves.
The first half is about how you work with AI. Not whether you use it (everyone does now), but whether you have a real workflow, real judgment, and real guardrails around it. The second half is the same set of fundamentals that have always mattered: the box model, the event loop, how the browser paints a frame. The twist is that these fundamentals matter more now, not less, because they are how an interviewer checks whether you understand the code an agent just wrote for you.
The 15 questions below, drawn from a full frontend mock-interview walkthrough, map that new shape almost perfectly. The first five are about your relationship with AI. The rest are about whether the machinery underneath still makes sense to you.
A note on how to read this: interviewers are not grading you on reciting definitions. They are watching for judgment. For every question, the definition gets you a passing grade and the tradeoff gets you the offer. I have tried to give you both.
Part 1: How you work with AI (Questions 1 to 5)
1. Build a button that scales on hover, efficiently
The word doing all the work here is “efficiently.” Anyone can write :hover { transform: scale(1.05); }. The interviewer wants to know if you understand why that is the right answer and what the wrong answers cost.
Scaling on hover should use transform: scale() and animate it with a transition, because transform and opacity are the only two properties the browser can animate on the compositor thread without triggering layout or paint. If instead you animate width, height, margin, or padding, every frame forces the browser to recalculate layout (reflow) and repaint, which is what produces janky, stuttering hovers.
A strong answer walks the rendering pipeline briefly (layout, then paint, then composite) and explains that transform skips the first two stages. Bonus points for mentioning prefers-reduced-motion so the animation respects accessibility settings, and for noting that will-change can hint the browser to promote the element to its own layer, but should be used sparingly because it costs memory.
If you are being asked this while driving an AI agent, the real test is whether you would catch the agent animating width and correct it.
2. What does your AI-assisted workflow look like beyond basic prompting?
This question separates people who use AI as fancy autocomplete from people who have rebuilt how they ship.
A credible answer has a few pillars. You manage context deliberately: you feed the agent the relevant files, the type definitions, the design system, and your conventions (often through a rules or config file the tool reads), rather than hoping it guesses. You work spec-first: you write a short plan before generating anything, so the agent is coding against a clear target. You keep the feedback loop tight: small diffs you can review, not thousand-line dumps. You let AI own the tedious 80 percent (boilerplate, tests, mechanical refactors) while you own architecture and the hard 20 percent. And you lean on guardrails (types, tests, linting) so nothing merges just because it looks plausible.
The red flag interviewers listen for is “I paste the error and ask it to fix the error until it works.” That is not a workflow, it is a slot machine. The strongest answers also mention knowing when not to reach for the agent at all.
3. How do you keep code quality high when using AI?
The accurate framing is that AI does not lower code quality or raise it. It raises the volume of code, and volume is what breaks weak quality systems.
So the answer is about the system, not the willpower. Automated gates first: strict TypeScript, ESLint, Prettier, and a real test suite running in CI, so AI-generated code has to clear exactly the same bar as anything a human writes. Small, reviewable pull requests, because a diff you cannot review is a diff you cannot vouch for. Conventions encoded where the agent can read them, so it produces code that matches your house style by default. And a firm rule: you do not merge code you do not understand.
The insight that lands well is that the bottleneck has moved. When writing code was the slow part, that was the constraint. Now generation is nearly free, so review is the constraint. Teams that stay fast are the ones that invest in fast, automated feedback so a human is not the thing slowing everything down.
4. How do you test AI-generated code?
Testing philosophy matters more here than any specific tool.
The core principle is unchanged: test behavior, not implementation. AI is very good at generating test scaffolding and at suggesting edge cases you would have forgotten, and that is worth using. The trap is that AI will also happily write tests that pass trivially, tests that just mirror the implementation, or tests that only exercise a mock. Those give you a green checkmark and no actual safety.
Structure the answer in layers: unit tests for pure logic, component and integration tests (React Testing Library) for user-visible behavior, and end-to-end tests (Playwright or Cypress) for the handful of flows that would be a disaster if they broke. Make clear that coverage percentage is not quality; a codebase can be 90 percent covered and still be tested badly.
The senior framing: you decide what “correct” means, and the tests are the specification the AI codes against. If your tests are sloppy, the AI will confidently build to a sloppy spec.
5. How do you keep token spend low when working with AI coding agents?
This is a cost-awareness question, and it doubles as a proxy for whether you use these tools at scale.
The biggest lever is context discipline. Most wasted spend comes from dumping an entire repository into the context window or re-explaining the same background on every turn. Give the agent only the files that matter for the task in front of it. Match the model to the job: a cheap, fast model for renaming and boilerplate, an expensive one reserved for hard reasoning. Scope tasks tightly so you are not paying for the agent to wander. Clear or compact the context between unrelated tasks so stale history is not riding along in every request. And write a good spec up front, because a clear prompt that gets it right once is far cheaper than ten cheap prompts that circle the problem.
The line that shows maturity: a clean prompt with the right three files beats a giant context window every time.
Part 2: Design systems and CSS (Questions 6 to 9)
6. What makes a good design system?
A weak answer describes a component library. A strong answer describes a product with internal customers.
A good design system starts with design tokens: colors, spacing, typography, radii, and shadows defined once as the single source of truth, so a brand change is one edit and not a thousand. On top of tokens sit composable, accessible primitives and components with a clear, predictable API. Around all of it: documentation, versioning, and a governance process for how things change.
But the part most people miss is adoption. The real measure of a design system is not how beautiful it is, it is whether using it is easier than bypassing it. A gorgeous system that teams route around is a failed system. So the best answers talk about developer experience, sensible defaults, and accessibility baked in at the primitive level rather than bolted on later.
In one sentence: a good design system encodes your team’s decisions so nobody has to re-litigate them, and it earns its adoption by being the path of least resistance.
7. Explain the CSS box model
This is a fundamental, and the interviewer mostly wants to hear that you have it internalized rather than memorized.
Every element on the page is a rectangular box made of four layers, from the inside out: the content, then the padding around it, then the border around that, then the margin outside everything. That is the box model.
The detail that separates a real answer is box-sizing. By default (content-box), the width you set applies only to the content, and padding and border are added on top, which means a width: 200px element with padding is actually wider than 200 pixels. This constantly surprises people. Setting box-sizing: border-box makes width include the padding and border, so the box is exactly the width you asked for. Most codebases set it globally for that reason.
Worth mentioning too: vertical margins between elements collapse into a single margin rather than adding up, which is another classic source of “why is there a gap there” confusion.
8. How does CSS specificity work?
Specificity is the algorithm the browser uses to decide which rule wins when several rules target the same element and set the same property.
From lowest to highest weight: element and pseudo-element selectors (like div or ::before) are weakest, then classes, attribute selectors, and pseudo-classes (like .card or :hover), then IDs, and finally inline styles beat all selectors. !important sits above even that and is best avoided. When two rules have equal specificity, the one that appears later in the source wins.
People often picture it as a tuple, roughly (inline, IDs, classes, elements), compared left to right.
What signals seniority is treating high specificity as a smell rather than a tool. Reaching for IDs and !important to win a fight usually means the architecture is fragile. The modern answers to this are cascade layers (@layer), which let you control precedence intentionally, and :where(), which wraps selectors at zero specificity so they are trivially easy to override. Both exist specifically to stop specificity wars.
9. What are the core principles of responsive design?
The classic pillars are fluid grids, flexible media, and media queries, but a 2026 answer should sound more current than a 2015 blog post.
The foundation is relative units over fixed pixels: percentages, rem, em, viewport units, and fr in Grid, so layouts stretch and shrink instead of breaking. Media is made flexible with rules like max-width: 100% so images never overflow their container. Layout is handled with Flexbox and Grid rather than floats. And you go mobile-first: write the base styles for small screens, then layer complexity upward with min-width queries, because it is easier to add than to unwind.
The details that show you have kept up: clamp() for fluid typography that scales smoothly between a minimum and a maximum without a single breakpoint, and container queries, which are the real evolution of responsive design. Media queries respond to the viewport; container queries let a component respond to the space it has been given, so the same card behaves correctly in a sidebar and in a full-width hero without knowing anything about the page around it.
Part 3: JavaScript internals and performance (Questions 10 to 13)
10. Give a high-level overview of the event loop
This is the question that reveals whether your mental model of asynchronous JavaScript is correct or superstitious.
JavaScript runs on a single thread with one call stack, so it can only do one thing at a time. The event loop is what lets it stay responsive anyway. Synchronous code runs on the call stack. When you hit something asynchronous (a timer, a network request, a DOM event), that work is handed off to the browser’s Web APIs, and your code keeps going. When the async work finishes, its callback is placed in a queue.
Here is the part interviewers wait for. When the call stack is empty, the event loop does not just grab the next thing at random. It first drains the entire microtask queue (Promise callbacks, queueMicrotask), then takes a single macrotask (a setTimeout callback, an event), then lets the browser render if needed, then repeats. Microtasks always have priority.
